aspects.name: Word meaning in literary excerpt
aspects.description: The student is shown an underlined word or phrase within a passage from a classic literary work (novel/play) and must choose the answer that most nearly captures its meaning AS USED in that specific context. Cognitive demand: disambiguate among plausible dictionary senses of an already-known word by attending to the surrounding literary cues.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 differs from A2 in that the target word is given (not blank) and the passage is always a literary excerpt with an authorial attribution; an A1 clone that drops the literary frame or removes the target word is a categorical drift to A2.

types.name: Single-word meaning (verb/adjective/noun)
types.description: Stem asks 'As used in the text, what does the word "X" most nearly mean?' The target X is a single, familiar English word whose contextual sense is one of its less-dominant dictionary meanings.
types.core_skill_demand: Reject the first/most-common sense of a familiar word and select the sense activated by surrounding clues (descriptive phrases, parallel structures, the speaker's tone).

characteristics: Target is a concrete adjective/noun with a clearly stated paraphrase nearby (e.g., 'how nice and soft it sounds — just as if someone was kissing the window')
Distractors are obviously off-topic dictionary senses (e.g., 'shapeless', 'bland' for 'soft')
Stimulus passage is short (≤3 sentences) with explicit framing prose
easy.example_stem: As used in the text, what does the word 'soft' most nearly mean? — Alice describes snow sounding 'nice and soft', 'just as if someone was kissing the window'.

characteristics: Target is a polysemous verb whose intended sense is metaphorical or idiomatic (e.g., 'assumed' = acquired; 'quality' = characteristic)
At least two distractors are valid dictionary senses of the target in other contexts
Contextual cue requires reading 1-2 surrounding sentences, not just the target line
medium.example_stem: Day by day, the work assumed greater precision … — what does 'assumed' most nearly mean?

characteristics: Target word has 3+ active dictionary senses, with two of them plausible in the passage
Correct answer is a near-archaic or specialized sense (e.g., 'contracted' = developed/acquired, 'endure' = tolerate, 'manifest' = perceptible)
Distractors include the most-common modern sense as a deliberate trap
hard.example_stem: [Mr. Brooke] was held to have contracted a too rambling habit of mind — what does 'contracted' most nearly mean? (Distractor 'Restricted' is the dominant modern sense.)

must_preserve: Literary excerpt framing with author/date/work attribution
Target word visibly marked (underline/quote) in the passage
Stem wording: 'As used in the text, what does the word "X" most nearly mean?'
Four single-word options, each capitalized as a standalone gloss
must_vary: Choice of target word (a different polysemous English word)
Literary source (different author/era/genre)
Identity of the trap distractor (most-common sense)
common_bugs: Picking a word whose intended sense IS its most common sense (collapses to trivial)
Using a modern non-literary passage (drifts into A2 territory)
Distractors that are all clearly wrong dictionary senses, removing the disambiguation challenge
Marking the target with brackets instead of underline/quote, breaking schema conventions

types.name: Multi-word phrase meaning
types.description: Stem asks about a 2-4 word phrase ('a singular', 'reconcile his mother to', 'disputing'-in-context as transitive action) whose compositional meaning differs from its parts.
types.core_skill_demand: Treat the phrase as an idiomatic unit and select a paraphrase that captures its specific contextual function, not the sum of its lexical parts.

characteristics: Phrase appears in a passage with explicit narrative support
Options are short paraphrases differing in clearly opposite directions
easy.example_stem: What does the phrase 'X' most nearly mean? (Easy literary excerpt with clear surrounding context.)

characteristics: Phrase has a literal reading that competes with its idiomatic reading
One distractor preserves the literal sense; correct answer captures the idiom
medium.example_stem: He had attempted to reconcile his mother to the notion — what does 'reconcile his mother to' most nearly mean? Answer: 'Get his mother to accept'.

characteristics: Phrase is used in an unusual transitive sense or extended metaphor
All four options are syntactically parallel and semantically near-neighbors
Correct answer requires inferring an extended/figurative meaning from scene-level imagery (e.g., 'disputing … our passage' = providing resistance to)
hard.example_stem: Here they were now, in blue, disputing, every inch, our passage — what does 'disputing' most nearly mean? Answer: 'Providing resistance to'.

must_preserve: Literary attribution and stimulus structure as in T1
Phrase (not single word) marked as the target
Stem wording: 'As used in the text, what does the phrase "X" most nearly mean?'
must_vary: Choice of phrase (different idiomatic/figurative construction)
Literary source
common_bugs: Choosing a phrase whose meaning is fully compositional (no idiomatic challenge)
Picking only the literal sense distractor as the answer (defeats the type)
Treating this as T1 by selecting a single-word target instead

aspects.name: Fill-in-the-blank vocabulary precision
aspects.description: The student is shown an informational/expository passage with one blank and must choose the most logical and precise word or phrase to complete it. Cognitive demand: identify the semantic relationship the surrounding context dictates, then pick the option whose precise sense fits that relationship.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 differs from A1 in that there is no underlined target — the student must SUPPLY the word given context cues; a clone that supplies an underlined word in a literary passage drifts to A1.

types.name: Restatement / elaboration cue
types.description: The sentence containing the blank is followed (or preceded) by an explicit restatement — typically via colon, semicolon, dash, or 'in other words / that is' — that defines what the blank must mean. The blank is a high-level summary word.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the explicit definitional gloss in the surrounding clause and select the single word that exactly summarizes it (without adding or removing connotation).

characteristics: Restatement is in the same sentence with a colon/dash and uses common vocabulary (e.g., 'calmer waters where animals can take shelter' defines 'tranquil')
Three distractors are clearly opposite or off-topic (dangerous, imaginative, surprising)
Correct answer is a high-frequency word ≤2 syllables in everyday register
easy.example_stem: Kelp forests … slow currents to create a more ______ environment with calmer waters where animals can take shelter.

characteristics: Restatement is present but in a separate sentence or buried behind a relative clause
Correct answer is a mid-register word (3-4 syllables: exactitude, marginalize, persistent)
At least one distractor is semantically adjacent (e.g., 'precariousness' vs. 'exactitude')
medium.example_stem: … inhabitants used natural landmarks to track time with a high degree of ______. By observing the sun's position … the Mexica were able to precisely identify the dates …

characteristics: Restatement is implicit via an example list or extended description (no colon)
Correct answer is a low-frequency academic word (ubiquity, anomaly, paucity, variable)
Two distractors are sophisticated synonyms-on-the-surface that fail on a specific nuance (e.g., 'heterogeneity' vs. correct 'ubiquity' when the cue stresses prevalence not variety)
hard.example_stem: Archaeologists have identified a plethora of beads … attribute these artifacts' apparent ______ to … widespread availability of T. gibbosula.

must_preserve: An explicit or near-explicit gloss in the stimulus that defines the blank
Stem wording: 'Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?'
Four single-word options of the same part of speech
Informational/expository register (no narrative)
must_vary: Subject domain (science, history, arts, social science) — sample broadly
Position of the restatement (before vs. after the blank)
Difficulty register of the correct word and distractors
common_bugs: Removing the gloss to make the question 'harder' — this turns it into pure vocabulary recall, not Words-in-Context
Picking a correct answer that adds connotation absent from the gloss (e.g., 'enthusiastic' when the gloss only says 'consistent')
Inserting two equally valid synonyms among the options (creates an ambiguous key)
Drifting register: easy stem + sophisticated correct word, or vice versa, breaks difficulty calibration

types.name: Contrast / concession cue
types.description: The stimulus sets up a contrast using 'despite', 'however', 'unlike', 'while', 'but', 'in contrast', or a counterintuitive 'though'. The blank requires a word that completes the contrasted side of the relationship.
types.core_skill_demand: Read the contrast signal, identify which pole the blank sits on, and select a word that is the appropriate antonym/contrast of the named pole — neither too weak nor too strong.

characteristics: Contrast signal is explicit ('Unlike X who did A, Y ______ A')
Required contrast is a direct antonym in common vocabulary (embraced vs. rejected)
Distractors include the SAME-pole synonym as a trap
easy.example_stem: Unlike Kuroda Seiki, who adopted traditional European methods, Hishida Shunsō ______ traditional Japanese approaches.

characteristics: Contrast spans two sentences or uses 'although' / 'while' with embedded clauses
Correct word is mid-register and one distractor is the contrast's exact opposite (overcorrecting)
Often involves a researcher/critic challenging a prevailing view
medium.example_stem: Although science fiction was dominated by white male authors when Butler began writing, she did not view the genre as ______.

characteristics: Contrast is implied by 'while recent scholarship has undermined claims that … were ______' — requires reconstructing what the disproved claim asserted
Correct word is academic register (dogmatic, inconsequential, opaque)
All four distractors are intellectually plausible; only one matches the precise direction of the contrast
hard.example_stem: While recent scholarship has undermined claims that the works of Ibn Rushd were ______ other Muslim philosophers of his time, … his works were primarily available thousands of miles west.

must_preserve: An explicit contrast/concession signal word in the stimulus
Two contrasting referents (entity A vs. entity B, or expectation vs. reality)
Standard fill-in stem
must_vary: The contrast signal used (despite / however / unlike / while / but)
Subject domain
Whether the blank sits on the 'expected' or 'unexpected' side
common_bugs: Picking a contrast word that is too strong or too weak relative to the named pole
Failing to flip polarity for 'less ______' or 'not ______' framings (creates inverted key)
Removing the contrast signal during cloning — collapses into T3
Two options that are valid antonyms of the named pole — ambiguous key

types.name: Cause / effect / function cue
types.description: The stimulus describes a causal chain, mechanism, or function and the blank is the verb (or verb-derived noun) naming the action performed in that chain. Often: 'X led to / helped to / caused ______'.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the directional action that the cause performs on the effect (or vice versa) and pick a verb whose connotation (positive/negative, weak/strong) matches the described mechanism.

characteristics: Cause-effect is explicit ('helped to ______ photography: they made it easy and enjoyable …')
Correct verb is common and directly entailed by the explanation
Distractors include obvious-wrong-direction verbs (weaken when text says made widely accessible)
easy.example_stem: Carte de visite photos helped to ______ photography: they made it easy and enjoyable for everyday people to have their pictures taken.

characteristics: Causal chain spans multiple clauses; the blank verb describes a researcher's or actor's intent
Correct verb is mid-register (rectify, mitigate, stimulate, indulge)
One distractor is a verb that names a related but distinct action (rectify vs. retain)
medium.example_stem: To ______ this situation, Maggie Lena Walker chartered the St. Luke Penny Savings Bank in 1903.

characteristics: Causal mechanism is described abstractly or counterintuitively ('counterintuitive effect of ______ demand')
Correct verb is low-frequency academic (engendering, stymie, exploited, mitigate)
Distractors include surface-plausible verbs that fail on direction OR on agent-fit (e.g., 'praise' fails because subject is inanimate)
hard.example_stem: The price of pop music cassette tapes rose dramatically, which had the counterintuitive effect of ______ demand: buyers thronged the market …

must_preserve: An explicit causal / mechanistic / functional relationship in the stimulus
The blank as a verb (or deverbal noun) naming the action
A directional cue (positive vs. negative outcome) that fixes the verb's polarity
must_vary: Domain (lab science, social science, history, arts)
Whether the actor is a person, an institution, or an inanimate process
Polarity of the relationship (promote vs. inhibit)
common_bugs: Picking a verb with wrong polarity (inhibit when context says promote)
Picking a verb that requires an animate agent for an inanimate subject (e.g., 'praise')
Weak-strong mismatch: 'rectify' when the stimulus only says 'address slightly'
Inserting two valid synonyms in the options

types.name: Degree / magnitude / quality descriptor
types.description: The blank is an adjective (or adjectival phrase) describing the quality, magnitude, or character of a noun, where the surrounding text supplies the measurable characteristic ('most ______', 'highly ______', 'extremely ______', 'a ______ process').
types.core_skill_demand: Read the descriptive details and pick the adjective whose specific connotation captures the dominant property the text emphasizes — not just 'positive' vs. 'negative' but the right facet (e.g., persistent vs. impartial, arduous vs. haphazard).

characteristics: Descriptive details directly entail the correct adjective ('great number and variety' → 'effective')
Distractors are obviously wrong adjectives
Adjective is a common positive/negative descriptor (effective, tranquil)
easy.example_stem: This method of farming proved to be highly ______, as evidenced by the great number and variety of crops grown at that time.

characteristics: Correct adjective requires nuance (competent vs. imaginative; integral vs. tangential)
Descriptive details support 2 of 4 options superficially
Adjective sits at the boundary between two qualities
medium.example_stem: Cuttlefish appear to be surprisingly ______ at exercising self-control: in a 2021 study … these cephalopods routinely demonstrated restraint by delaying gratification …

characteristics: Adjective is academic/literary (arduous, opaque, peripheral, disparate, unassuming)
Stimulus suggests qualities by example/analogy rather than naming them
At least two distractors are sophisticated near-synonyms differing on a single feature (e.g., 'contentious' vs. 'arduous')
hard.example_stem: The creation of Lotte Reiniger's 1926 animated film was ______ process. Over the course of three years, Reiniger painstakingly made more than 250,000 individual images …

must_preserve: Adjective slot (with appropriate degree modifier when present: 'most', 'highly', 'a/an ______')
Descriptive support sentences that uniquely entail the correct adjective's facet
must_vary: The specific facet emphasized (effort, scope, character, attitude)
Domain
common_bugs: Choosing a generically-positive adjective when text specifies a particular positive facet
Degree mismatch (very-strong adjective when text supports only mild quality)
Distractors with same valence but different facets — ambiguous key
Forgetting to flip polarity when the degree modifier negates ('less ______', 'not particularly ______')

types.name: Two-word / prepositional collocation
types.description: The blank is filled by a two-or-more word phrase consisting of a verb/adjective + preposition (or other relational word): 'contingent on', 'overshadowed by', 'demarcated from', 'mediated by', 'a proponent of', 'irrelevant to'. The task tests both the head word's meaning and its idiomatic preposition.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the relational meaning required (dependence, contrast, comparison, exclusion) AND verify the idiomatic preposition matches the head word in standard English.

characteristics: Phrase uses common verbs/adjectives plus standard preposition ('inspired by', 'a proponent of')
Context strongly fixes the relationship direction
easy.example_stem: Carmen Lomas Garza … is ______ direct experience, drawing from memories of her childhood …

characteristics: Head word is mid-register (contingent on, depleted of, saturated with)
One distractor uses a similar head word with a different preposition (susceptible to vs. saturated with)
medium.example_stem: What a photograph conveys is therefore largely ______ how it is balanced.

characteristics: All four options are two-word phrases of identical syntactic shape
Correct head word is academic (mediated by, demarcated from, independent of, overshadowed by)
At least one distractor is a real English collocation that fails on directional polarity (e.g., 'predictive of' vs. 'independent of')
Frequently the stimulus presents a researcher's nuanced claim about how X relates to Y
hard.example_stem: Nuisance costs … are largely ______ donation size, so charities will likely favor a few large donors over many small donors.

must_preserve: Two-or-more-word phrase options, all of identical syntactic shape (verb+prep or adj+prep)
Idiomatic prepositional pairings that are standard English
Context cue that fixes the relational direction
must_vary: Head word and preposition (vary both)
Direction of relationship
common_bugs: Mixing single-word and multi-word options — breaks the type
Picking a phrase with non-idiomatic preposition (e.g., 'consistent on' instead of 'consistent with')
Two options that are valid for the directional cue — ambiguous key
Reducing during cloning to a single-word answer — drifts to T3/T5/T6

types.name: Negation / scarcity / counterfactual framing
types.description: The blank appears inside a negated, comparative-less, or counterfactual frame: 'not ______', 'less ______', 'unlikely to be ______', 'lacked ______', 'a ______ of [studies]'. The student must select a word whose meaning, once negated by the frame, yields the relationship the stimulus describes.
types.core_skill_demand: Mentally invert the frame to recover what is being denied or said to be scarce, then pick a word whose face value (before negation) matches that denied content.

characteristics: Rare at easy difficulty; when present, the frame is a simple 'not ______' with strong context
Correct word is common and the polarity flip is one step
easy.example_stem: (rare; if present, e.g., 'These plants are not ______ to deep water — they are harmed by it.')

characteristics: Frame uses 'not' or 'less' with mid-register vocabulary (atypical, innocuous)
Correct word's face value is the OPPOSITE of what the stimulus describes as actual
medium.example_stem: Rydra Wong … is a poet, an occupation which, in Delany's work, is not ______: nearly a dozen of the characters are poets or writers.

characteristics: Frame is non-trivial: 'lacked ______ explanation', 'unlikely to be ______', 'paucity of', 'a ______ account', 'by no means ______'
Correct word is academic and its negation yields the precise descriptor (e.g., 'lacked a straightforward explanation' means the explanation is complex)
Distractors include words that fit only without the negation (a trap for readers who miss the frame)
hard.example_stem: We are unlikely to ever have ______ account of ancient Egypt under Hatshepsut, as much of the evidence … was deliberately destroyed.

must_preserve: An explicit negation, scarcity, or counterfactual frame around the blank
Stimulus content that, once the frame is applied, fixes the answer unambiguously
must_vary: The specific frame device (not / less / unlikely to be / paucity of / by no means)
Domain
common_bugs: Picking a word that fits the stimulus content directly, ignoring the negation (inverted key)
Removing the negation during cloning — collapses to T3 or T6
Stacking two negations carelessly so the reader cannot parse intended polarity
Selecting an option that fits the frame but contradicts the supporting evidence

skill_wide_notes: Across the bank, hard items in A2 almost always rely on either (a) low-frequency academic vocabulary (T3-hard, T6-hard, T7-hard) or (b) a syntactic frame the student must invert before applying meaning (T4-hard contrast, T7-hard relational phrases, T8-hard negation). Easy items concentrate in T3 (explicit gloss) and T5 (explicit causal link) with high-frequency vocabulary. Note: T8 (negation framing) is essentially absent at Easy difficulty — this is a hardness lever in itself. T2 (multi-word phrase meaning) is rare (only 3 originals) but clearly distinct from T1. A clone preserving aspect+type but inflating vocabulary register without preserving the underlying cognitive frame will produce false difficulty inflation; verifiers should check that the structural cue (gloss / contrast / cause / frame) is preserved at the targeted difficulty's specified strength.
